title: The Product Quality Review (PQR/APQR) Explained
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A senior QP guide to the product quality review (PQR/APQR): EU GMP scope, trending under ICH Q9, QP and MAH duties, and how to make it a real tool.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, with a single subtle green accent. The foreground shows a quality reviewer's hands resting beside a sleek tablet displaying an elegant statistical control chart and trend curves, alongside a row of softly out-of-focus glass vials and a bound dossier on a polished dark surface. Soft natural light rakes from the left, shallow depth of field blurring the laboratory backdrop into smooth navy bokeh, calm and authoritative mood, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a PQR and an APQR?
faqs.a: There is no substantive difference; the terms are used interchangeably. PQR (Product Quality Review) is the wording used in EU GMP Chapter 1, while APQR (Annual Product Quality Review) is common industry shorthand reflecting the typical twelve-month review period. The US annual review under 21 CFR 211.180(e) serves the same purpose with slightly narrower defined scope.

faqs.q: How often must a product quality review be performed?
faqs.a: EU GMP requires the review to be conducted regularly, which in practice means annually for most licensed products, though a different justified frequency can be defended on a risk basis. Critically, the review should also be completed in a timely manner after the period closes, so that any adverse trend can still be acted upon. A review finalised many months late is a common inspection finding.

faqs.q: Who is responsible for the product quality review under contract manufacturing?
faqs.a: The manufacturer or importer normally compiles the review, but the Marketing Authorisation Holder retains an independent duty to verify it has been carried out and is accurate. The technical agreement must define who provides which data and who performs the assessment. The Qualified Person should have visibility of the conclusions, as the review supports ongoing assurance that the process remains in control.
